title: Leverage Effect: WSE vs NYSE
text: This project investigates the correlation between returns and volatility in equity markets. It compares the leverage effect (the increase in volatility that follows a negative return period) on the Warsaw Stock Exchange (WSE) and the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE). The analysis is based on daily data for a sample period of 10 years, from 2016 to 2026.

title: Leverage Effect: WSE vs NYSE
text: This project investigates the correlation between returns and volatility in equity markets. It compares the leverage effect (the increase in volatility that follows a negative return period) on the Warsaw Stock Exchange (WSE) and the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE). The analysis is based on daily data for a sample period of 10 years, from 2016 to 2026.

title: Leverage Effect: WSE vs NYSE
text:
Motivation and source
The analysis is inspired by an academic article on quantitative analysis of the leverage effects in financial markets.

Reference article: Reigneron, P.-A., Allez, R., & Bouchaud, J.-P. (2011). Principal regression analysis and the index leverage effect.
Project goal: replicate the original analysis for S&P 500 index components, adapt the methodology to Polish WIG30 index components, compare the nature of stock price correlations between the two markets.

title: Leverage Effect: WSE vs NYSE
text:
Tools

Python (libraries: pandas, numpy, matplotlib, seaborn, yfinance)
Jupyter Notebook for data analysis and visualization

title: Leverage Effect: WSE vs NYSE
text:
Data

Stock price data: Yahoo Finance (via Python library yfinance)
Frequency: daily
Number of companies: 30 (for WIG30) and 500 (for S&P 500)
Sample period: April 2016 to April 2026 (update of the original period used for 2025 conference)


Preprocessing

select the close prices and merge data for all stocks in each index
manage missing observations dates-wise and company-wise
calculate log returns
normalize returns

title: Leverage Effect: WSE vs NYSE
text:
Methodology
Define:

\(r_{\alpha}(t)\): return of stock \(\alpha\) at time \(t\), \(r^2_{\alpha}(t)\): volatility of stock \(\alpha\) at time \(t\)
\(I(t) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{\alpha=1}^N r_{\alpha}(t)\): average return at time \(t\) (,,synthetic index return’’), \(I^2(t)\): proxy for index volatility at time \(t\)
\(\sigma^2(t) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{\alpha=1}^N r^2_{\alpha}(t)\) average stock volatility at time \(t\)
\(\rho(t) = \frac{1}{N(N-1)} \sum_{\alpha \neq \beta} r_{\alpha}(t) r_{\beta}(t)\): average correlation of stock returns at time \(t\)

The authors propose two facts about the index volatility:

\(I^2(t)\) depends on past returns: \(I^2(t) = I^2_0 +  a(\tau) \cdot I(t-\tau)\), where \(\tau\) is the time lag
market fluctuations are driven by both the volatility of individual stocks and their correlations: \[I^2(t) \approx \rho(t) \sigma^2(t)\]

To quantify these observations, the authors propose to regress \(I^2(t)\), \(\sigma^2(t)\), and \(\rho(t)\) on past returns \(I(t-\tau)\), for different lags \(\tau\). The regression linear coefficients are \(a(\tau)\), \(b(\tau)\), and \(c(\tau)\), respectively.

title: Leverage Effect: WSE vs NYSE
text:
Results
In both markets we observe the leverage effect, because the correlation between past returns and current volatility is negative. KEy differences

title: Actuarial Projects Portfolio
text:
I am building a portfolio of projects related to actuarial science, quantitative methods, and computational modelling. The repository combines academic work with coding-based investigations, including simulation, statistical analysis, and financial modelling.




Comparative analysis of volatility asymmetry in the Polish and U.S. equity markets, motivated by academic literature and supported by empirical market data.
